data.title: Market Voices: Trump picks Warsh; Exxon, Chevron on Venezuela
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks.

  • President Trump has selected former Federal Reserve Gov. Kevin Warsh as his nominee to succeed Jerome Powell as Fed chair.

"I have known Kevin for a long period of time, and have no doubt that he will go down as one of the GREAT Fed Chairmen, maybe the best. On top of everything else, he is 'central casting,' and he will never let you down. Congratulations Kevin!" Trump wrote in a post on Truth Social. 

  • Republican Sen. Thom Tillis, who sits on the Senate Banking Committee, said he plans to block Warsh's nomination until the Trump administration ends its criminal investigation into the Fed and Fed Chair Jerome Powell.

“I am pleased with the nominee; I think that by most accounts he’s well regarded,” Tillis told Bloomberg TV. “But we still have to clear the current matter,” Tillis said, adding that the investigation was “frivolous prosecution.”

Powell, who has been harshly criticized by Trump in recent months, is slated to step down as Fed chair at the end of May.

“We have some time between now and May for that investigation to go away and for the confirmation process to open up,” Tillis said.

  • Exxon Mobil (XOM) CEO Darren Woods said the Venezuelan government needs to implement major reforms before his company could consider investing in the country's oil industry. 

“Those priorities start with one, stabilizing the country,” Woods told CNBC. “Second is to kickstart the economy and try to recover some of the damage that’s been done over the decades of abuse that the dictators brought in, and then ultimately to transition into representative government.”

  • Meanwhile, Chevron (CVX), which continued to operate in Venezuela during the Maduro regime, said the government has taken positive steps to help protect investments in its oil industry.

In an interview with CNBC, Chevron CEO Mike Wirth said that contract security, commercial stability, and regulatory predictability were needed in order for the country to attract additional  investment.

“We see Venezuela taking steps in a positive direction to address those issues, which will encourage investment, not only from a company like ours but from others that I think are also considering the opportunities there,” Wirth told CNBC.

“We’ve stayed when others didn’t,” Wirth added. “We’re getting paid back some debts that we’re owed, and it does give us a significant head start. And the country has tremendous long-term potential.”

data.title: Moderna announces departure of medical chief
data.description:
Moderna (MRNA) announced on Friday that the company’s chief medical officer, Jacqueline Miller, will resign effective Mar. 2, a little over a year after taking over the role.

The Cambridge, Massachusetts-based company added that David Berman, who most recently served as R&D head at Immunocore (IMCR), will become the company's Chief Development Officer, effective the same date.

Miller previously served as the COVID-19 vaccine maker’s Senior Vice President, Therapeutic Area Head for Infectious Diseases, for more than four years before becoming the medical chief in November 2024.

She will also resign as a member of the company’s Executive Committee but will serve Moderna (MRNA) as a consultant to support the transition, the company said. Meanwhile, Berman will also become a member of the company’s Executive Committee and report to Chief Executive Officer Stéphane Bancel.

His appointment comes as Moderna (MRNA) pivots to newer mRNA-based medications such as personalized cancer therapies as demand for its COVID-19 vaccine declines.

“Over a two-decade career, Dr. Berman has worked on more than a dozen clinical-stage immunotherapies, including senior leadership roles in developing four oncology biologics,” Bancel said.

“I am deeply grateful for Jackie's leadership and significant contributions over the past five and a half years,” he added.

data.title: Chili's sizzle is eating fast food's lunch
data.description:
What started in the mid-1970s as a small Texas burger, taco, and chili spot has grown into Chili’s (EAT), a worldwide phenomenon known for its margaritas, baby back ribs, and its outsized contribution to its parent company’s earnings.

Founded by Larry Levine in 1975 in a converted post office outside Dallas, Texas, and named for the chili on its menu, Chili’s was bought in 1983 by Norman Brinker and became part of Brinker International’s (EAT) portfolio.

Today, there are more than 1,200 Chili’s restaurants in the U.S. with another 400 spread out across the globe—including the Middle East, Asia, and South America—making it the third most popular casual dining chain in the U.S. (after Texas Roadhouse and Olive Garden).

With the help of a generous advertising budget and a famous earworm jingle for its ribs, the company’s growth exploded, surpassing rival Applebee’s with $1M more sales per store and contributing the vast majority of Brinker International’s (EAT) $5.4B in annual revenue.

More specifically, the chain’s meteoric growth was driven by successful menu upgrades, restaurant upgrades, and effective social media marketing—including a viral “cheese pull" video—that lured in Gen Zers, Millennials, Baby Boomers, and everyone else in between.

And at a time when consumers grew increasingly frustrated by the high prices at fast food chains, Chili’s capitalized on its value proposition. With average checks approaching those at McDonald’s (MCD), diners reconsidered casual dining as a more affordable option.

As a result, Chili’s same-store sales grew an average of 12% between 2022 and 2025, outpacing the casual dining segment by as much as 680 basis points.

The turnaround can be credited to not just the menu, mozzarella sticks, and memorabilia, but also Kevin Hochman, who became Brinker’s (EAT) chief executive in 2022. Hochman went to work by focusing on three “”T's”—Traffic, Tickets, and Team. He reduced the number of menu items by 25%, reinvested in marketing (leaning into social media), and invested in staff and reduced the complexities in the kitchen (embracing the “Chilihead” culture).

“The Chili's turnaround is real, it is sustaining, and we have no intentions of taking our foot off the gas, which means we will continue to be focused on improving our food, service, and atmosphere, as well as continue making Chili's more fun, easier, and more rewarding for our team members,” Hochman said on the company’s most recent earnings call. 

And to sustain that growth, Chili’s is capitalizing on consumers’ shift from expensive beef to more affordable chicken with a “super premium chicken sandwich” lineup to be debuted in April with a “substantial” ad campaign.

“Chicken sandwiches is a very large market with over 80% of people buying them at least once last year and is by far the biggest segment of all restaurant chicken servings,” Hochman added. Even chains like McDonald’s (MCD) that were once heavily associated with burgers are now seeing a noticeable shift into chicken, selling equal amounts of burgers and chicken tenders.

This bodes well for Chili’s future growth, as the company has clearly shown its skill in luring in diners with inexpensive and tasty food and keeping the pressure on its fast-food rivals.

As consumers continue to trade down in search of value, Chili’s recent gains underscore how operational discipline and targeted marketing have helped the brand regain relevance, positioning it as a key driver of Brinker’s performance heading into the next fiscal year.

data.title: Wall Street's decline intensifies as tech trade sell-off deepens; Warsh picked as new Fed chair
data.description:
Wall Street's main averages were significantly lower on Friday as investors continued to assess the latest company earnings and technology stocks carried on declining.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.8% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.9%, and the Dow (DJI) was -1%.

All indexes are on track for weekly declines. Both the S&P 500 ((SP500)) and the Dow ((DJI)) are expecting monthly gains, but the Nasdaq ((COMP:IND)) might see losses on the month.

“Bulls have reason to smile: a positive January often sets the tone for a bullish year. When the S&P 500 (SP500) finishes the month higher, history shows full-year gains averaging 16.9% since 1950,” said Ryan Detrick, chief market strategist at Carson Group.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver (XAGUSD:CUR) prices continued to lower, now with the precious metals back around the $4,980/oz and $93/oz levels, respectively.

Investors turned their focus on U.S. President Donald Trump's selection of Kevin Warsh as the Federal Reserve chair.

“I have a tough time seeing Kevin Warsh being able to persuade his colleagues to a dovish position, a position that elevates employment risks over inflation and one he has never been able to make himself, even when the times demanded,” said Neil Dutta, head of economic research at Renaissance Macro.

“Markets tend to test new Fed chairs. The average correction in the first six months is 15%. When the chair faces their first crisis, investors don't wait and see how they handle it. Fed independence is a potential crisis for Warsh, but it could be anything,” said Ed Clissold, chief U.S. strategist for Ned Davis Research.

In other economic news, the producer price index came in hotter than expected in December. Also, the Chicago PMI rose far more than expected in January to 54.0, up from 42.7 in the prior month.

On earnings, Apple (AAPL) released its first quarter fiscal 2026 financial results that were driven by record-breaking iPhone sales. Shares were last -1.3%.

Also, Visa's (V) fiscal Q1 2026 earnings and revenue topped the average analyst estimates, but their stock was last -2.8%. KLA (KLAC) shares also dipped -12.6% even after its Q2 results topped estimates.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point higher at 4.25%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was 2 basis points lower at 3.55%.

data.title: The Bancorp targets $5.90 EPS for 2026 and $8.25 for 2027 with fintech initiatives and buybacks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Bancorp, Inc. (TBBK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Damian Kozlowski announced a new brand for The Bancorp, describing it as “a bold representation of the exciting future in front of us.” He reported, “The Bancorp earned $1.28 a share in the fourth quarter. EPS growth year-over-year was 11%.” Kozlowski highlighted that GDV increased 16% for the quarter versus the prior year, while credit sponsorship balances ended at $1.1 billion, up 40% from the third quarter and 142% year-over-year. He stated the company “exceeded our goal of at least $1 billion in credit sponsorship balances,” and is aiming to add at least two new partners in the coming year.
  • Kozlowski indicated that the embedded finance platform is on track for launch early in 2026 and that Cash App program implementation should meaningfully benefit GDV and fee revenue in 2026 and beyond. He added, “All 3 initiatives should be an increasingly positive effect on our financials as we move through '26 and show significant impact as we enter '27.”
  • Kozlowski reported criticized assets declined from $268 million to $194 million quarter-over-quarter and delinquency fell from 2.19% to 1.6% of loans.
  • CFO Dominic Canuso stated, “ROE was a record 30.4% in the quarter and 28.9% for the full year, continuing the trend of year-over-year improvements.” He detailed that ending assets increased to $9.4 billion, up 7% versus prior year and that “noninterest income, when excluding the credit enhancement, account for just over 30% of revenue in the quarter, with approximately 90% of fees coming from the fintech business.” Canuso also noted that net charge-offs and provision for loans both declined significantly quarter-over-quarter. He added, “Noninterest expense for the quarter was $56.2 million and included $2 million from a legal settlement relating to a previously disclosed legal proceeding.” The company repurchased $150 million of stock in the quarter, totaling $375 million for the year.

Outlook

  • Kozlowski announced, “We are initiating guidance of $5.90 EPS for 2026. We are targeting at least $1.75 a share in the fourth quarter 2026. We are maintaining a preliminary guidance for '27 of $8.25 a share.” He stated that 2026 buybacks are forecasted at $200 million total or $50 million a quarter. Kozlowski highlighted the expected impact of fintech initiatives, platform restructuring, AI tools, and continued buybacks as key EPS drivers, with gains subject to fintech development timelines and stock price for buybacks.

Financial Results

  • The Bancorp earned $1.28 per share in Q4 2025, with year-over-year EPS growth of 11%. GDV rose 16% for the quarter versus prior year. Revenue growth for the quarter, excluding credit enhancement income, was 3% year-over-year. For the full year, GDV grew 17% and total fee growth reached 21%. Ending assets increased to $9.4 billion, and the total loan portfolio increased $919 million to $7.26 billion, driven by $644 million in consumer fintech loans. Average deposits for the quarter were $7.6 billion with an average cost of 177 basis points. Provision for loans was $858,000, down from $5.8 million in the previous quarter, and net charge-offs were $629,000, down from $3.3 million. Noninterest expense was $56.2 million, including a $2 million legal settlement.

Q&A

  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: Asked for detail on EPS ramp and key drivers. Kozlowski responded that “they're large revenue opportunities,” and that there is now “more clarity... than we did last -- end of last quarter where we did not issue preliminary guidance for 2026,” expressing confidence in meeting the $175 million target for the end of 2026.
  • Yanchunis: Asked about drivers weighing on Q4 results. Canuso cited legal fees, the government shutdown’s impact on payments and deposits, and timing of credit sponsorship growth, most of which occurred late in the quarter.
  • Yanchunis: Queried about off-balance sheet deposits. Canuso explained the strategy is to optimize earnings and monetize excess liquidity as deposit growth continues.
  • Emily Noelle Lee, KBW: Asked about REBL book refinancings and the Aubrey property. Canuso noted some refinancings involved new partners and lower yields, with properties in stronger positions. Regarding Aubrey, Canuso said the property is nearing cash flow breakeven and expects to seek an exit after stabilization, while Kozlowski emphasized a prudent exit for maximum value.
  • Lee: Asked about fintechs obtaining bank charters. Kozlowski stated, “We don't see an impact right now on our portfolio, and we don't expect to have a major impact in the future,” citing scalability and cost advantages.
  • Lee: Probed further into off-balance sheet deposit economics. Canuso indicated revenue generation will increase as deposit growth outpaces loan growth.
  • Unknown Analyst, Oppenheimer: Asked about $102 million in criticized REBL loans. Kozlowski described a diversified portfolio mostly in Southeast states, with no single market concentration.
  • Yanchunis: Sought outlook for credit sponsorship portfolio. Kozlowski projected it could “easily be double where we are today at the end of '26.” Canuso discussed expected NIM compression as the fintech mix grows, with profitability increasing as fee revenue rises.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally probing, focusing on the sustainability of EPS guidance, drivers of Q4 results, off-balance sheet deposit strategy, and risks in real estate and fintech portfolios. The tone was neutral, with clarifying follow-up questions and requests for transparency on key variables.
  • Management’s tone during prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing new guidance, fintech momentum, and credit quality improvements. In the Q&A, executives provided detailed explanations, expressing confidence in guidance and business model resilience, with Kozlowski stating, “We're very clear on the... program implementation guidelines, we now can better predict where we're going to be at the end of the year.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautious (no 2026 guidance previously) to confident, with specific EPS targets and more clarity on fintech initiatives. Analysts’ tone was consistent, seeking specifics on execution and risk but not overtly skeptical.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company shifted from providing no specific 2026 guidance in Q3 to issuing detailed EPS targets for 2026 and 2027 in Q4. Strategic focus advanced from foundational fintech initiatives to execution and scaling, with a new brand launch and progress on embedded finance. Analysts shifted from seeking clarity on program launches (e.g., Cash App) and criticized asset resolution in Q3 to drilling into EPS trajectory, deposit strategy, and fintech portfolio growth in Q4. Management’s confidence increased, now offering precise targets and timelines. Key metrics such as credit sponsorship balances and criticized assets showed marked improvement from the previous quarter, and the company is now signaling material balance sheet optimization to support fintech growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged some Q4 headwinds, including unexpected legal fees and the government shutdown’s impact on payments and deposits. Timing of credit sponsorship growth was also cited as a factor, with most occurring at the end of the quarter. Executives expressed confidence that these factors are largely behind them and do not expect a repeat in Q1 2026. In real estate, management stressed prudent exit strategies for difficult assets and portfolio diversification to manage risk. No major threats were seen from fintechs seeking bank charters, given the company’s platform scalability and cost advantages. Management highlighted ongoing liquidity optimization and balance sheet flexibility to support fintech expansion.

Final Takeaway

The Bancorp, Inc. closed 2025 with strong earnings momentum, issuing new EPS targets of $5.90 for 2026 and $8.25 for 2027, supported by fintech initiatives, embedded finance launch, and continued stock buybacks. Management reported substantial progress in credit sponsorship and asset quality, while conveying confidence in meeting ambitious growth and profitability goals through focused execution and platform scalability.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Centerra suspends operations at Pennsylvania plant after explosion
data.description:
Centerra Gold (CGAU) -12% in Friday's trading as mining stocks slide across the board alongside precious metal futures, and the company said it suspended operations at its Langeloth metallurgical facility in Pennsylvania following an explosion that injured four workers.

Centerra (CGAU) said the accident was the result of an uncontrolled mixture of chemicals leading to an uncontained chemical reaction adjacent to the acid plant; the company said it is assessing the extent of the damage and the potential duration of the shutdown.

The Langeloth plant forms part of Centerra’s (CGAU) molybdenum business unit in the U.S. and Canada.

data.title: HSBC moves to the sidelines on Pinterest as job cuts point to a 'softer near-term'
data.description:
HSBC Global Investment Research on Friday downgraded social media platform Pinterest Inc. (PINS) to "hold" from a previous investment rating of "buy," citing a softer near-term outlook after the company cut jobs by 15% to reallocate resources to AI-focused roles.

The research firm said the company's renewed AI push, coming without any indication of cost savings or revenue acceleration and requiring a trade-off in resources, reflects a tier-2 player struggling to keep up with larger and better-resourced peers that are running ahead.

"While we continue to see competitive value in PINS’ differentiated user data set, we find that the growing gulf in reach, engagement, and resources vs. Big Tech platforms will likely prove increasingly difficult to bridge," HSBC said in its note.

"PINS’ main profit-generating N. America region has been a particular sore point as ARPU grew only 6% y-o-y over 9M25, lagging behind peers despite multi-year investments in AI capabilities by PINS that span advertiser, creator, and consumer tools and features," they added.

PINS' price target was cut from $34.50 to $24.90, now implying an upside of 11.4%. Stock has lost nearly a third of its value in the past 12 months.

data.title: Aon Q4 earnings top consensus, extending beat streak to three quarters
data.description:
Aon (AON) on Friday posted Q4 earnings that topped Wall Street expectations, extending its run of quarterly beats to three in a row, although revenue fell short.

The insurance broker also issued full-year 2026 guidance, calling for mid-single-digit or greater organic revenue growth (vs. +6% in FY25), as well as 70-80 basis points of adjusted operating margin expansion, and double-digit free cash flow growth (vs. +14% in FY25).

Q4 adjusted EPS of $4.85, exceeding the $4.75 consensus, advanced from $4.42 in the year-ago quarter.

Q4 total revenue of $4.30B, vs. $4.37B expected, drifted up 4% from $4.15B a year earlier. The decline reflects 5% organic revenue growth and a 2% favorable impact from foreign currency translation, partially offset by a 3% unfavorable impact from acquisitions, divestitures and other items.

AON shares edged up 0.5% in midday trading.

Total operating expenses rose 1% to $3.1B Y/Y in Q4.

Free cash flow was $1.32B in the quarter ended Dec. 31, 2025, up 16% Y/Y.

Adjusted operating margin of 35.5% at the end of Q4 compared with 33.3% at the end of Q4 2024.

"Our strategic investments in data-driven insights and capabilities through Aon Business Services are enabling us to meet rising client demand in an increasingly complex environment," President and CEO Greg Case said in a statement. "We are entering 2026 with momentum and are well positioned to continue to deliver for our clients, generate sustainable growth and create long-term shareholder value."

data.title: Charter outlines post-Cox integration strategy with plans for $1,000 annual customer savings guarantee and declining CapEx through 2028
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Charter Communications (CHTR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Christopher Winfrey reported that Charter added nearly 2 million mobile lines in 2025, representing 19% growth, and described the company as "the fastest-growing mobile provider in the United States." He highlighted that video customer losses were dramatically reduced, and video customers grew in the fourth quarter, attributing improvements to "video product improvements we've made over the past 2 years." Winfrey noted, "Our revenue was down about 0.5% in 2025, driven by customer losses and a challenging political advertising comparison, while EBITDA grew by about 0.5%."

  • Winfrey detailed strategic priorities for 2026: "Assuming regulatory approval of Cox, Spectrum will cover over 70 million households," expanding scale, product development, and savings opportunities. He also announced plans to "nearly complete our rural build-out, providing us with over 1.7 million new subsidized rural passings" and said, "By the end of this year, 50% of the current spectrum network will be upgraded to symmetrical and multi-gig service."

  • Winfrey introduced the "invincible WiFi" product, set to launch in February, combining WiFi 7, 5G, and battery backup, and announced a savings guarantee: "We'll now guarantee you $1,000 of savings per year when you take Internet and 2 lines of mobile from Spectrum. If we can't save you $1,000 or more...we'll credit the difference on your bill during the first year."

  • CFO Jessica Fischer explained new reporting methodologies, integrating all mobile customers into customer statistics and introducing "total connectivity customers." Fischer reported, "Including residential and small business, we lost 119,000 Internet customers in the fourth quarter, better than last year's fourth quarter." She added, "We added 428,000 [mobile] lines...Net adds in the quarter were lower due to heavy device subsidy activity by the big telco competitors."

Outlook

  • Winfrey stated, "I'm not projecting broadband relationship growth this year, but we expect to see an improved trajectory from the investments we've made over the past 3 years." He forecasted EBITDA growth: "From a financial perspective, we expect our operating plan to deliver EBITDA growth this year."

  • Fischer projected, "For the full year 2026, we are planning for slight EBITDA growth, excluding the impact of transition costs." She noted capital expenditures guidance: "We expect total 2026 capital expenditures to reach $11.4 billion" and anticipated "capital intensity to return to 13% to 14% of revenue by 2028 at Charter stand-alone."

  • Fischer disclosed a new leverage target: "We are moving our post-transaction target leverage to the low end of a new 3.5 to 3.75x range, which we expect to achieve within 3 years following close."

Financial Results

  • Fischer stated, "In total, consolidated fourth quarter revenue was down 2.3% year-over-year and down 0.4% when excluding advertising revenue and programmer app allocation."

  • Adjusted EBITDA declined by 1.2% year-over-year in the quarter, but for the full year 2025, EBITDA grew by 0.6%.

  • Net income attributable to Charter shareholders in the fourth quarter was $1.3 billion, compared to $1.5 billion in the prior year period.

  • Capital expenditures in the fourth quarter totaled $3.3 billion, with $11.66 billion for the full year 2025. Fischer said, "We expect total 2026 capital expenditures to reach $11.4 billion."

  • Free cash flow for the fourth quarter was $773 million, about $200 million lower than last year.

  • Charter repurchased 2.9 million shares for $760 million during the fourth quarter.

Q&A

  • Craig Moffett, MoffettNathanson LLC, asked about the new Verizon agreement and offload strategy. Winfrey replied, "We've amended and modernized our long-term MVNO agreement with Verizon...We have a structural and long-term agreement that underpins everything that we're doing here, and that hasn't changed." On offload, he stated, "I think it's around 89% or something like that's bumping in that area. So it's moved up a bit, but it's on a steady climb."

  • Ben Swinburne, Morgan Stanley, questioned the market position of Spectrum Mobile. Winfrey said, "The convergence strategy is working...Our brand awareness continues to go up every year. The reputation of the product continues to improve and settle in."

  • Vikash Harlalka, New Street Research, asked about market share in fiber-competitive areas. Winfrey responded, "We have greater penetration than our fiber competitors, even in mature fiber markets."

  • Jessica Reif Cohen, BofA, inquired about the sustainability of video subscriber gains. Winfrey explained, "Our goal is to have a Video product that supports broadband acquisition and broadband retention."

  • Michael Ng, Goldman Sachs, asked about balancing EBITDA growth with investment. Winfrey answered, "We've made the investments...That gives us the ability to have increasing efficiency through the business and still be able to innovate."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral to slightly positive, with a focus on competitive positioning, market share, and financial discipline. Questions pressed for sustainability of improvements and competitive differentiation.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing operational improvements, product innovation, and capital allocation discipline. During Q&A, responses were thorough, occasionally cautious when addressing competitive threats and growth projections.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone was similarly focused on competitive and financial dynamics, while management's tone became more forward-looking and explicit about strategic guarantees and capital plans.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize guarantees for customer savings and a new post-transaction leverage target, while the previous quarter focused on maintaining capital discipline and preparing for the Cox integration.

  • Strategic focus expanded from AI-driven efficiencies to explicit guarantees on service and savings, and new product launches like invincible WiFi.

  • Management introduced integrated customer reporting, reflecting a convergence focus. Analysts continued to probe for evidence of sustainable growth and margin improvement.

  • Key metrics reflected ongoing declines in revenue and net income, but management projected stabilization and future growth, especially in free cash flow as capital expenditures decrease.

Risks and Concerns

  • Winfrey cited a "challenging political advertising comparison" and customer losses as primary drivers of revenue decline.

  • Competition from expanded cell phone Internet, higher mobile substitution, and fiber overlap was described as ongoing headwinds, with Winfrey noting, "Collectively, that drove fourth quarter Internet sales slightly lower year-over-year."

  • Fischer highlighted the impact of heavy device subsidy activity by telco competitors, resulting in lower net mobile adds.

  • Programming costs and churn remain concerns, with management reiterating the importance of innovative product packaging and operational efficiency as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Charter management emphasized product innovation, cost discipline, and customer guarantees as core strategies for 2026, underpinned by the completion of major capital projects and the pending Cox integration. The company signaled that while immediate broadband relationship growth is not projected, operational improvements, new service guarantees, and capital expenditure reductions are expected to drive long-term EBITDA and free cash flow growth, with a focus on delivering tangible value to both customers and shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lilly announces $3.5B Pennsylvania plant for next-gen obesity drugs
data.description:
• Eli Lilly (LLY) on Friday announced plans to invest more than $3.5B to build a new manufacturing facility in Lehigh Valley, Pennsylvania, to develop its next-generation weight-loss medicines, including its "triple G" agonist retatrutide.
  • The manufacturing facility will focus on producing the company’s next-generation weight-loss therapies, including retatrutide, the Indiana-based drugmaker said, adding that it is the fourth U.S. production site it has announced since February 2025.
  • Eli Lilly (LLY) plans to begin its construction work this year, generating 2,000 construction jobs. The injectable medicine and device site is expected to launch in 2031, creating 850 high-value jobs in the area.
  • The pharma giant has already posted Phase 3 data showing that retatrutide, a GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon triple hormone receptor agonist, can generate up to 24% of weight loss.
